original_reasoning: Arabic خزعل: halting limping gait (خزعال = lameness). Greek σχολαῖος: leisurely, slow pace. Both indicate impeded/reduced movement speed.
Arabic خزق: to pierce/impale sharply, to split open. Greek ἄνδιχα: asunder/in twain. Both involve forcibly dividing through material.
Arabic داف (دأف): to finish off/dispatch a prisoner (kill). Greek δάπτω: to devour, as wild beasts. Both involve violent lethal action on prey/victim.
خسق includes sinking into or opening the ground under hoof or tread, and χάσκω is to gape open; the chain is fairly clear.
Arabic means a man much exposed or uncovered; Greek means be left behind, yielding a plausible uncovered-leftover conceptual chain.
Spoiling and becoming foul after cleansing maps plausibly onto being undone or dissolved.
مَحارِث work by cutting earth; Greek 'carve, cut, engrave' preserves the cutting action directly.
ἁλιπόρφυρος means sea-purple or purple-colored; Arabic رفاهة centers on ease, luxury, and refined comfort, which matches the prestige-luxury side of purple.
